agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a pure infrastructure optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is fundamentally incorrect and dangerous.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact for a shipment lifecycle — includes sender, recipient, timestamps, proof of delivery, customs declarations, weight/dimensions, and chain-of-custody events."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The business rule governing how long delivery records are stored before deletion. This is NOT purely an infrastructure concern — it encodes legal, contractual, and operational obligations."},{"term":"Compliance Reporting","definition":"The use of historical delivery data by B2B customers to satisfy their own regulatory and audit obligations (e.g., VAT filings, customs audits, supply chain due diligence)."},{"term":"Right to Erasure","definition":"GDPR Article 17 right allowing data subjects to request deletion of personal data, subject to exceptions for legal obligations."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered — often legally significant for disputes, insurance claims, and contractual fulfillment."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The minimum period a business is legally required to retain certain records, which overrides any internal deletion policy."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records containing VAT-relevant data must be retained for the minimum statutory period required by the applicable EU member state tax authority (typically 5-10 years depending on jurisdiction).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit VAT records going back multiple years. Premature deletion makes the company unable to respond to audits, resulting in penalties and presumed liability.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record with VAT-relevant data WHEN the statutory retention period has not elapsed THEN the record MUST NOT be auto-deleted."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records involving customs declarations for cross-border shipments must be retained per EU Customs Code requirements (minimum 3 years, up to 10 years in some member states).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customs authorities require access to historical import/export records. Destruction of these records can constitute a legal violation.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record with customs declaration data WHEN the customs retention period has not elapsed THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the period specified in the customer's service agreement, as they depend on this data for their own compliance reporting.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that B2B customers contractually rely on for their compliance reporting constitutes a breach of contract and exposes both parties to regulatory risk.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a B2B customer delivery record WHEN the contractual retention period has not elapsed THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Delivery records that are subject to an active dispute, legal hold, or insurance claim must never be auto-deleted regardless of retention policy.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence relevant to active legal proceedings or disputes constitutes spoliation and creates severe legal liability.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record under legal hold or active dispute WHEN auto-deletion is triggered THEN the deletion MUST be blocked."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Personal data within delivery records is subject to GDPR data minimization principles — retention must be justified by a lawful basis and limited to what is necessary.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation. Retaining personal data longer than necessary is itself a violation, but so is deleting data required for legal obligations.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record containing personal data WHEN no lawful basis for retention exists AND all statutory periods have elapsed THEN the record should be eligible for deletion or anonymization."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record exists with VAT-relevant data in an EU jurisdiction requiring 7-year retention","when":"The auto-deletion process runs at 90 days","then":"The record MUST NOT be deleted; it must be retained until the statutory period expires."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A B2B logistics customer relies on 3-year-old delivery data for their annual compliance audit","when":"The 90-day auto-deletion policy is applied retroactively","then":"The data MUST still be available; deletion must respect contractual SLAs."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery record is under legal hold due to an active dispute","when":"Any auto-deletion policy runs","then":"The record must be excluded from deletion entirely."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A B2C delivery record where all statutory retention periods have genuinely elapsed and no disputes are active","when":"The retention period expires","then":"The record should be deleted or anonymized in compliance with GDPR data minimization."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A cross-border delivery with customs declaration data","when":"90-day auto-deletion is triggered","then":"The record MUST NOT be deleted; customs retention requirements (3-10 years) take precedence."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"B2B customer requests historical delivery data at month 4 for their tax audit, but data was already deleted at day 91.","expected_behavior":"This MUST NOT happen. The platform is contractually and potentially legally obligated to provide this data. This scenario represents a breach of contract and a compliance failure for the B2B customer."},{"scenario":"A consumer files a dispute about a delivery 6 months after it occurred.","expected_behavior":"Proof of delivery and delivery record must still exist to resolve the dispute. If deleted at 90 days, the platform loses its evidence and likely bears the loss."},{"scenario":"Tax authority audits the company's VAT records for shipments from 3 years ago.","expected_behavior":"All VAT-relevant delivery records must be producible. Inability to produce them results in penalties and presumed tax liability."},{"scenario":"GDPR subject access request for a delivery that occurred 2 years ago.","expected_behavior":"If the data was retained for lawful purposes (tax, contractual), it must be producible. If it was deleted at 90 days, the platform cannot comply with the SAR for retained-for-legal-basis data, which is actually correct if no lawful basis existed — but problematic if it was deleted prematurely while a lawful basis DID exist."},{"scenario":"A B2B customer churns but their historical delivery data is still within statutory retention periods.","expected_behavior":"Data must be retained per statutory requirements regardless of customer relationship status. Contract termination does not override legal retention obligations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Premature deletion of VAT and customs records exposes the company to tax authority penalties, customs fines, and inability to defend audits. B2B contract breaches trigger SLA penalties and potential lawsuits. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine (GDPR fines up to 4% of global annual turnover, tax penalties vary by jurisdiction).","operational":"CRITICAL. B2B customers who depend on the platform for compliance reporting will be unable to fulfill their own regulatory obligations, causing mass churn. Customer support will be unable to resolve delivery disputes older than 90 days. Internal finance and legal teams lose access to records needed for audits and litigation.","user":"B2C consumers lose ability to reference or dispute deliveries after 90 days. B2B customers face compliance failures in their own businesses. Trust in the platform as a reliable data custodian is destroyed.","risk":"EXTREME. This change creates simultaneous exposure across tax law, customs regulations, GDPR, contract law, and commercial liability. The risk is not hypothetical — EU tax audits routinely go back 5+ years. A single customs audit or tax investigation after data deletion could result in penalties far exceeding any storage savings.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Cannot perform compliance reporting, tax filings, or customs audits using platform data. Breach of contractual obligations. Likely mass churn."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Cannot reference or dispute deliveries older than 90 days. Loss of proof of delivery for warranty claims, insurance, returns."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team (Internal)","impact":"Cannot produce records for VAT audits. Company exposed to presumed liability and penalties."},{"group":"Legal Team (Internal)","impact":"Cannot defend disputes, litigation, or regulatory inquiries involving historical deliveries. Spoliation risk."},{"group":"Compliance / DPO (Internal)","impact":"Must balance GDPR minimization against statutory retention — 90 days satisfies neither correctly. Requires a nuanced, per-record-type policy, not a blanket rule."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"The actual solution is far more complex than a simple TTL — requires classification of records by retention category, legal hold flags, and per-jurisdiction rules."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because critical regulatory and contractual details specific to this platform were never provided. However, the general domain knowledge about EU logistics data retention requirements is well-established and makes it near-certain that 90-day blanket deletion violates multiple legal obligations. The exact statutory periods vary by jurisdiction and record type, which we do not have.","high_confidence_areas":["90-day blanket deletion will violate EU VAT record retention requirements","B2B customers will lose access to data they contractually depend on","This is NOT a pure infrastructure change — it is a business logic and compliance change","Legal hold and active dispute records must never be auto-deleted"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per EU member state for this platform's specific jurisdictions","Specific contractual retention commitments in B2B customer agreements","Whether the current 7-year policy was set based on legal review or is itself arbitrary","Customs record retention requirements for specific shipment types handled by this platform","Whether any data anonymization strategy could satisfy both GDPR minimization and statutory retention"],"assumptions":["The platform handles VAT-relevant transactions as part of delivery billing","B2B customer contracts include data availability SLAs or retention commitments","The platform processes cross-border shipments subject to customs regulations","No legal review was conducted before this proposal was made"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review has been provided for this proposed change. EU and UK data retention requirements for logistics records are jurisdiction-specific and record-type-specific. A blanket 90-day policy cannot be evaluated without knowing: (1) which specific EU member states the platform operates in, (2) what record types are stored (VAT invoices, customs declarations, PODs), (3) what contractual retention commitments exist in B2B agreements, and (4) whether legal counsel has reviewed this proposal.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","B2C Consumers","Finance/Tax Team","Legal Team","Compliance/DPO","Engineering Team"],"options":["REJECT the 90-day blanket deletion proposal until legal review is completed","Commission a per-jurisdiction, per-record-type retention analysis","Implement tiered retention: anonymize PII early, retain legally required fields for statutory periods","Engage legal counsel to define minimum retention periods by record category and jurisdiction"],"recommendation":"REJECT this change immediately. Do not proceed without a formal legal review. The correct approach is a tiered, per-record-type retention policy that satisfies both GDPR minimization AND statutory retention obligations. Storage cost optimization should target anonymization and cold storage tiering, not premature deletion."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"B2B customer contractual obligations regarding data retention and availability SLAs were never provided. These contracts may contain explicit retention commitments that make 90-day deletion a breach of contract.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Legal Team","Sales/Account Management"],"options":["Review all B2B contracts for data retention clauses before any policy change","Consult with B2B customers about their retention needs","Grandfather existing contracts and apply new policy only to new agreements"],"recommendation":"Audit all B2B customer contracts for retention-related clauses before proceeding. B2B customers who use platform data for compliance reporting almost certainly have contractual expectations around data availability."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The engineering team's framing of this as 'no business logic changes' suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of the domain implications. It is unclear whether any business stakeholder, legal counsel, or compliance officer was consulted before this proposal.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering Team","Product Management","Compliance/DPO"],"options":["Require cross-functional review (legal, compliance, product, finance) for any data lifecycle changes","Establish a formal change control process for retention policies"],"recommendation":"Mandate that all data retention policy changes go through legal and compliance review before reaching engineering. This is not an infrastructure decision."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Blanket 90-day deletion of delivery records in EU/UK logistics operations will almost certainly violate statutory record retention requirements (VAT, customs, commercial law).","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2"},{"violation":"B2B customers who depend on delivery data for compliance reporting will lose access to legally required records, constituting breach of contract and causing downstream compliance failures.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"No legal hold or dispute exemption mechanism was mentioned in the proposal, risking destruction of evidence in active legal matters.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"The change was incorrectly classified as 'infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes' — this is a material change to business rules, legal compliance, and contractual obligations.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4, BR-5"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a pure infrastructure optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is fundamentally incorrect and dangerous.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifec

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data retention but also data minimization
2. UK GDPR post-Brexit similar requirements
3. B2B customers use this data for compliance reporting - 90 days is almost certainly too short
4. Tax/customs regulations in EU typically require 5-10 year retention
5. Dispute resolution, chargebacks, insurance claims often need historical data


6. The claim that this involves "no business logic changes" is misleading—reducing retention from years to 90 days fundamentally alters how the system handles compliance and customer obligations.

The real problem is that critical information is missing from this proposal: nobody's specified the actual regulatory requirements that apply, whether legal has reviewed this, what compliance obligations B2B customers depend on, or what contractual commitments exist with them.
